title: Getting started

title: Model validation
text: examples use simple frequency modelling setup based MTPL2.

text: Poisson models, standard practice check whether variance assumption broadly appropriate. dispersion ratio 1 indicates observed variance exceeds variance implied Poisson model. automatically invalidate model, provide important diagnostic signal. pricing practice, overdispersion may indicate: omitted heterogeneity model misspecification clustering data unmodelled portfolio structure

title: Pricing principles
text: Exposure measures amount risk observed portfolio, typically time coverage. practice, exposure equals policy-years: one year –> exposure = 1 six months –> exposure = 0.5 motor insurance, often expressed vehicle-years. exposure –> credible observations less exposure –> volatile outcomes Key principle Pricing metrics expressed per unit exposure.

title: Pricing principles
text: Insurance losses typically decomposed two components: frequency: number claims per unit exposure severity: average claim size , risk premium derived: risk premium = expected loss per unit exposure can written : risk premium = frequency × severity equivalently: risk premium = total loss / exposure risk premium also referred : pure premium burning cost represents expected cost claims, excluding expenses margins.

title: Pricing principles
text: Separating frequency severity useful : driven different risk factors often require different model assumptions behave differently across segments Typical modelling choices: frequency → Poisson GLM severity → Gamma GLM risk premium → derived modelled directly practice, approaches used: separate frequency/severity models direct burning cost model

title: Pricing principles
text: Pricing just estimating expected losses. process typically consists four steps: Exploration Analyse risk factors identify patterns data Estimation Fit statistical models (typically GLMs) Refinement Adjust coefficients ensure: stability monotonicity commercial acceptability Translation Convert model output tariff structure Key principle refinement step actuarial judgement plays key role.

title: Pricing principles
text: fitting models, essential understand data. factor_analysis() provides structured way analyse: frequency severity risk premium exposure Example: helps answer questions : differences segments credible? segments low exposure? patterns stable driven noise?

Trade-offs include: accuracy vs stability granularity vs interpretability statistical fit vs commercial constraints example: highly flexible model may overfit noise perfectly smooth tariff may ignore real risk differences role actuary balance aspects.

what: Risk differentiation as a core principle
title: Pricing principles
text: central objective pricing risk differentiation: higher-risk segments → higher premiums lower-risk segments → lower premiums Well-calibrated differentiation improves: portfolio quality predictability results alignment price risk Poor differentiation leads : adverse selection cross-subsidisation unstable performance

title: Pricing principles
text: Pure statistical output rarely suitable direct use tariffs. : data can sparse certain segments models can capture noise instead signal coefficients may fluctuate across adjacent levels Refinement introduces structure: smoothing reduces volatility monotonicity enforces logical consistency restrictions incorporate business rules goal “improve model”, ensure: tariff behaves predictable explainable way.

title: Refinement workflow
text: insurance pricing, model estimation usually final step. fitted GLM may capture structure portfolio well, raw coefficients always directly suitable implementation tariff. Common reasons include: irregular local variation lack monotonicity externally imposed tariff structures expert judgement directly represented model implementation constraints policy administration systems reason, pricing workflows typically distinguish : model estimation tariff refinement final refit pricing structure insurancerating formalises staged refinement workflow: fit unrestricted model initialise refinement object prepare_refinement() add one refinement steps inspect steps refit call refit() obtain final fitted model separation makes workflow easier understand, reproduce, audit.

title: Refinement workflow
text: Refinement used estimated model output statistically acceptable, yet suitable tariff structure. Typical use cases include: smoothing rating factor derived continuous variable imposing monotonicity restricting coefficients predefined relativity structure introducing expert-based relativities within existing model levels simplifying final tariff practical implementation cases, refinement applied premium model, rather separate frequency severity models. purpose refinement primarily smooth intermediate model components, impose structure final pricing signal used tariff.

title: Refinement workflow
text: workflow starts standard premium modelling setup: analyse continuous variable GAM convert tariff classes fit frequency severity models combine premium proxy fit unrestricted premium model refinement, inspect unrestricted coefficient structure:  stage, coefficients reflect unrestricted model fit. practice, output may already informative, still irregular detailed direct tariff use.
code: library(insurancerating) library(dplyr)  age_policyholder_frequency <- riskfactor_gam(   data = MTPL,   nclaims = "nclaims",   x = "age_policyholder",   exposure = "exposure" )  clusters_freq <- construct_tariff_classes(age_policyholder_frequency)  dat <- MTPL |>   mutate(age_policyholder_freq_cat = clusters_freq$tariff_classes) |>   mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor)) |>   mutate(across(where(is.factor), ~ biggest_reference(., exposure)))  freq <- glm(   nclaims ~ bm + age_policyholder_freq_cat,   offset = log(exposure),   family = poisson(),   data = dat )  sev <- glm(   amount ~ zip,   weights = nclaims,   family = Gamma(link = "log"),   data = dat |> filter(amount > 0) )  premium_df <- dat |>   add_prediction(freq, sev) |>   mutate(premium = pred_nclaims_freq * pred_amount_sev)  burn_unrestricted <- glm(   premium ~ zip + bm + age_policyholder_freq_cat,   weights = exposure,   family = Gamma(link = "log"),   data = premium_df ) rating_table(burn_unrestricted) #>          level               risk_factor est_burn_unrestricted exposure #> 1  (Intercept)               (Intercept)          1.073720e+04       NA #> 2            0                       zip          3.739250e-01      207 #> 3            1                       zip          1.000000e+00    11081 #> 4            2                       zip          7.575359e-01     7783 #> 5            3                       zip          7.325754e-01     7588 #> 6      [18,25] age_policyholder_freq_cat          2.167946e+00     1331 #> 7      (25,32] age_policyholder_freq_cat          1.488489e+00     3649 #> 8      (32,39] age_policyholder_freq_cat          1.205259e+00     4247 #> 9      (39,84] age_policyholder_freq_cat          1.000000e+00    17358 #> 10     (84,95] age_policyholder_freq_cat          5.868964e-01       72 #> 11          bm                        bm          9.980489e-01       NA  rating_table(burn_unrestricted) |>   autoplot()

title: Refinement workflow
text: Refinement begins : rating_refinement object stores: fitted base model underlying model data refinement steps added workflow point, model refitted. refinement object represents proposed tariff adjustment structure, yet final fitted result. distinction deliberate: refinement steps can inspected incorporated final model.
code: ref <- prepare_refinement(burn_unrestricted) ref #> <rating_refinement> #> Base model: glm, lm #> Steps: 0

title: Refinement workflow
text: Smoothing used rating factor derived continuous variable contains undesirable local variation. example, coefficient pattern : age 30–34 lower age 34–38 higher age 38–42 lower may statistically possible, undesirable tariff. Smoothing imposes stable structure rating factor.

title: Refinement workflow
text: key arguments : x_cut: binned factor present GLM x_org: original continuous variable breaks: preferred commercial cut points smoothing: smoothing specification weights: optional weighting, typically exposure
code: ref <- ref |>   add_smoothing(     x_cut = "age_policyholder_freq_cat",     x_org = "age_policyholder",     breaks = seq(18, 95, 5),     weights = "exposure"   )

title: Refinement workflow
text: plot belongs pre-refit workflow. shows: original fitted coefficients proposed smoothed structure purpose inspect refinement step , incorporated final fitted model.
code: print(ref) #> <rating_refinement> #> Base model: glm, lm #> Steps: 1 #> 1. smoothing [age_policyholder_freq_cat] autoplot(ref, variable = "age_policyholder_freq_cat")

title: Refinement workflow
text: Typical smoothing choices : "spline": polynomial-style smoothing "gam": flexible smooth curve "mpi": monotone increasing "mpd": monotone decreasing appropriate choice depends pricing context. example: age may justify flexible smooth insured value power may require monotonic relationship low-exposure tails may benefit exposure weighting

title: Refinement workflow
text: Restrictions used coefficients must follow predefined structure. Typical examples include: bonus-malus systems governance-approved relativities externally mandated tariff structures implementation constraints policy systems Restrictions differ smoothing: smoothing reshapes fitted pattern restriction imposes user-defined coefficients

title: Refinement workflow
text: restriction table must contain exactly two columns: original factor levels adjusted coefficients
code: zip_df <- data.frame(   zip = c(0, 1, 2, 3),   zip_adj = c(0.8, 0.9, 1.0, 1.2) )  ref <- ref |>   add_restriction(restrictions = zip_df)

title: Refinement workflow
text: shows proposed restricted structure relative original fitted model.
code: autoplot(ref, variable = "zip")

title: Refinement workflow
text: cases, fitted model uses broad factor level, business portfolio knowledge suggests granular differentiation required. example, model may estimate one coefficient “construction”, pricing practice distinguishes : residential construction commercial construction civil engineering particularly relevant subgroup exposure limited estimate stable coefficients directly.

title: Refinement workflow
text: normalize = TRUE, relativities scaled exposure-weighted average remains equal 1 within original level. preserves original model signal introducing finer structure.
code: relativities_activity <- relativities_list(   split_level(     "construction",     c("residential_construction", "commercial_construction"),     c(1.00, 1.15)   ) )  ref <- ref |>   add_relativities(     risk_factor = "business_activity",     risk_factor_split = "business_activity_split",     relativities = relativities_activity,     exposure = "exposure",     normalize = TRUE   )

title: Refinement workflow
text: Refinement steps alter part model structure. changes applied, remaining coefficients may also need adjust. reason, workflow end add_smoothing() add_restriction(). final step : refits model incorporating refinement steps.
code: burn_refined <- refit(ref)

title: Actuarial Workflow for Insurance Pricing Models
code: library(insurancerating)  # Factor analysis fa <- factor_analysis(   MTPL,   x = "zip",   nclaims = "nclaims",   exposure = "exposure",   severity = "amount" )  autoplot(fa, show_plots = 1:3) # Fit model mod <- glm(   nclaims ~ zip,   offset = log(exposure),   family = poisson(),   data = MTPL )  rating_table(mod) ##   risk_factor       level   est_mod ## 1 (Intercept) (Intercept) 0.1402024 ## 2         zip           0 1.0000000 ## 3         zip           1 1.0254064 ## 4         zip           2 0.9238016 ## 5         zip           3 0.9757522 # Refine coefficients zip_df <- data.frame(   zip = c("0", "1", "2", "3"),   zip_adj = c(0.90, 0.95, 1.00, 1.10) )  mod_refined <- prepare_refinement(mod) |>   add_restriction(zip_df) |>   refit()  rating_table(mod_refined) ##   risk_factor       level est_mod_refined ## 1 (Intercept) (Intercept)        0.136653 ## 2     zip_adj           0        0.900000 ## 3     zip_adj           1        0.950000 ## 4     zip_adj           2        1.000000 ## 5     zip_adj           3        1.100000

title: Extract model data — model_data
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(insurancerating) library(dplyr)  # Fit GAM for claim frequency age_policyholder_frequency <- riskfactor_gam(data = MTPL,                                              nclaims = "nclaims",                                              x = "age_policyholder",                                              exposure = "exposure")  # Determine clusters clusters_freq <- construct_tariff_classes(age_policyholder_frequency)  # Add clusters to MTPL portfolio dat <- MTPL |> mutate(age_policyholder_freq_cat = clusters_freq$tariff_classes) |> mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor)) |> mutate(across(where(is.factor), ~biggest_reference(., exposure)))  # Fit frequency and severity model freq <- glm(nclaims ~ bm + age_policyholder_freq_cat, offset = log(exposure),             family = poisson(), data = dat) sev <- glm(amount ~ bm + zip, weights = nclaims,            family = Gamma(link = "log"), data = dat |> filter(amount > 0))  # Add predictions for freq and sev to data, and calculate premium premium_df <- dat |> add_prediction(freq, sev) |> mutate(premium = pred_nclaims_freq * pred_amount_sev)  # Fit unrestricted model burn_unrestricted <- glm(premium ~ zip + bm + age_policyholder_freq_cat, weights = exposure, family = Gamma(link = "log"), data = premium_df)  # Impose smoothing and refit model burn_restricted <- burn_unrestricted |> add_smoothing(x_cut = "age_policyholder_freq_cat", x_org = "age_policyholder", breaks = seq(18, 95, 5)) |> refit_glm()  # Extract model data model_data(burn_restricted) } # }

title: Truncated cumulative distribution function — ptrunc
text: truncated CDF defined : $$ F_{trunc}(q) = \frac{F(\min(\max(q, ), b)) - F()}{F(b) - F()} $$ \(F\) CDF original distribution. function ensures numerical stability checking truncation interval lies within support distribution.

text: data data.frame containing insurance portfolio. nclaims Character, name column data number claims. x Character, name column data continuous risk factor. exposure Character, name column data exposure. amount (Optional) Character, column name data claim amount. Required model = "severity". pure_premium (Optional) Character, column name data pure premium. Required model = "burning". model Character string specifying model type. One "frequency", "severity", "burning". Default "frequency". round_x (Optional) Numeric value round risk factor x multiple round_x. Can speed fitting factors many levels.

title: Generalized Additive Model for Insurance Risk Factors — riskfactor_gam
text: list class "fitgam" following elements: prediction data frame predicted values confidence intervals. x Name continuous risk factor. model model type: "frequency", "severity", "burning". data Merged data frame predictions observed values. x_obs Observed values continuous risk factor.

title: Generalized Additive Model for Insurance Risk Factors — riskfactor_gam
text: Frequency model: Fits Poisson GAM number claims. log exposure used offset expected number claims proportional exposure. Severity model: Fits lognormal GAM average claim size (total amount divided number claims). number claims included weight. Burning cost model: Fits lognormal GAM pure premium (risk premium). Implemented aggregating exposure-weighted pure premiums. functionality still experimental.

title: Generalized Additive Model for Insurance Risk Factors — riskfactor_gam
text: function fit_gam() deprecated version 0.8.0 replaced riskfactor_gam(). addition name change, interface also changed: fit_gam() used non-standard evaluation (NSE), column names passed unquoted (e.g. x = age_policyholder). riskfactor_gam() uses standard evaluation (SE), column names must passed character strings (e.g. x = "age_policyholder"). makes function easier use programmatic workflows. fit_gam() still available backward compatibility emit deprecation warning removed future release.

Find active rows on different dates dates0 <- data.frame(active_date = seq(ymd("2014-01-01"), ymd("2014-05-01"), by = "months")) rows_per_date(portfolio, dates0, df_begin = begin1, df_end = end, dates_date = active_date) #>       begin1        end termination exposure premium car_type index_df #> 1 2014-01-01 2014-03-14  2014-03-14   0.2025     125      BMW        1 #> 2 2014-01-01 2014-05-10  2014-05-10   0.3583     150    TESLA        2 #> 3 2014-01-01 2014-03-14  2014-03-14   0.2025     125      BMW        1 #> 4 2014-01-01 2014-05-10  2014-05-10   0.3583     150    TESLA        2 #> 5 2014-01-01 2014-03-14  2014-03-14   0.2025     125      BMW        1 #> 6 2014-01-01 2014-05-10  2014-05-10   0.3583     150    TESLA        2 #> 7 2014-01-01 2014-05-10  2014-05-10   0.3583     150    TESLA        2 #> 8 2014-01-01 2014-05-10  2014-05-10   0.3583     150    TESLA        2 #>   active_date index_dates #> 1  2014-01-01           1 #> 2  2014-01-01           1 #> 3  2014-02-01           2 #> 4  2014-02-01           2 #> 5  2014-03-01           3 #> 6  2014-03-01           3 #> 7  2014-04-01           4 #> 8  2014-05-01           5  ## With extra identifiers (merge claim date with time interval in portfolio) claim_dates <- data.frame(claim_date = ymd("2014-01-01"), car_type = c("BMW", "VOLVO"))  ### Only rows are returned that can be matched rows_per_date(portfolio, claim_dates, df_begin = begin1,    df_end = end, dates_date = claim_date, car_type) #>        begin1        end termination exposure premium car_type index_df #>        <Date>     <Date>      <Date>    <num>   <num>   <char>    <int> #> 1: 2014-01-01 2014-03-14  2014-03-14   0.2025     125      BMW        1 #>    claim_date index_dates #>        <Date>       <int> #> 1: 2014-01-01           1  ### When row cannot be matched, NA is returned for that row rows_per_date(portfolio, claim_dates, df_begin = begin1,    df_end = end, dates_date = claim_date, car_type, nomatch = NA) #>        begin1        end termination exposure premium car_type index_df #>        <Date>     <Date>      <Date>    <num>   <num>   <char>    <int> #> 1: 2014-01-01 2014-03-14  2014-03-14   0.2025     125      BMW        1 #> 2: 2014-01-01 2014-05-10  2014-05-10   0.3583     150    TESLA        2 #>    claim_date index_dates #>        <Date>       <int> #> 1: 2014-01-01           1 #> 2:       <NA>          NA
